proposals.title: The Hyde Park Tower
proposals.subtitle: London's first free public skyscraper
proposals.tagline: Go up to give the ground back.
proposals.description: A slender 170m glass tower housing a free observation deck, exhibition galleries, a climate research centre, a culinary institute, co-working spaces, a 400-seat auditorium, and a summit restaurant — all with free public entry to the core spaces. 86% of the site is returned to wildflower meadow and native parkland. The tower occupies just 625 sqm of the 4,385 sqm site.
proposals.designPhilosophy: Based on KPF President James von Klemperer's 'Higher Purpose' thesis: that tall buildings should serve civic purposes, not just commercial ones. References KPF's One Vanderbilt (civic base, public summit), North Bund Lot 91 (all-electric, carbon-neutral), and the Crystal Palace precedent — Hyde Park hosted the most ambitious building of 1851.

annualGVA.source: Modelled on Tate Modern (£100m GVA) scaled for mixed-use programme and workspace tenants

directJobs.source: Estimated from comparable institutions: Tate (2,000 across 4 sites), British Museum (1,200)

annualVisitors.source: Conservative estimate: The Shard draws 2.3m at £32/ticket. A free tower would exceed this.

annualRevenue.source: 10-stream diversified model: F&B (£8m), workspace (£6m), events (£5m), sponsorship (£4m), donations (£4m), membership (£3.5m), exhibitions (£3m), retail (£2.5m), education (£2m), DCMS grant (£8m)

greenSpace.note: 86% of the site returned to park — more green than exists today

entryPrice.note: Observation deck, permanent galleries, and ground floor all free to enter

proposals.title: The London School of Making
proposals.subtitle: A public design academy and fabrication workshop
proposals.tagline: Learn to make things. In public. At the edge of London's greatest park.
proposals.description: A 4-storey cross-laminated timber building — the largest mass timber public building in London — housing a publicly accessible ground-floor workshop, professional design school, artist residencies, materials library, and rooftop productive garden. The ground floor is entirely open: visitors watch craftspeople work, try a lathe, or attend a drop-in class. The building itself is a teaching tool — every beam, joint, and cross-brace is exposed.
proposals.designPhilosophy: Draws from KPF's educational portfolio and their belief that architecture and urbanism are inseparable. The 80m-long deep timber colonnade on Bayswater Road is a monumental public gesture — inviting you in, making the activity inside visible from the bus. The building is transparent about its construction, its purpose, and its values.

annualGVA.source: Modelled on Central Saint Martins (King's Cross) revenue and creative economy multipliers

directJobs.source: Teaching staff (35-50), technicians (20-30), resident artists (15-25), admin (20-25), café/retail (20-30), facilities (15-20)

totalJobs.source: Direct + indirect, plus 800-1,200 graduates entering creative economy annually after year 5

annualVisitors.source: Building Centre draws 200k on a fraction of this footprint. Open workshop model drives repeat visits.

annualRevenue.source: Course fees, corporate workshops, venue hire, retail, café

greenSpace.note: Productive growing space and outdoor teaching areas on the roof

entryPrice.note: Ground floor workshop and exhibitions free. Courses are paid.

proposals.title: The Hyde Park Cultural Dock
proposals.subtitle: Gallery, workshops, market, and studios — a cultural village at the park gates
proposals.tagline: See the park through the building.
proposals.description: Four interconnected brick-and-bronze volumes rising 2–4 storeys, arranged around a central covered atrium and cut through by two pedestrian lanes that frame views of Kensington Gardens. Ground floor: a major gallery, artisan workshops, destination restaurant, and design bookshop. Upper floors: studios, co-working, and a 150-seat lecture theatre. The brick references the demolished perimeter wall — evolution, not erasure.
proposals.designPhilosophy: Draws directly from KPF's Covent Garden masterplan (London) and K11 Musea (Hong Kong) — the 'cultural commerce' model where art, retail, and food activate a public realm. The pedestrian lanes cutting through the massing are a direct reference to KPF's Ilsan Cultural Center in Seoul, where 'streets' were introduced into the complex to create urban permeability.

annualGVA.source: Modelled on KPF Covent Garden estate economics (rental income grew from £54m to £95m over a decade under KPF masterplan)

directJobs.source: Gallery/curatorial (25-35), artisan workshops (60-80), F&B (80-100), events (20-30), retail (30-40), operations (25-35), education (15-20)

totalJobs.source: Direct + supply chain and local economy multiplier

annualVisitors.source: Coal Drops Yard at King's Cross as primary comparable

annualRevenue.source: Retail rents, gallery income, F&B, events hire, workspace rents

greenSpace.note: South edge opens directly to Kensington Gardens with no barrier

entryPrice.note: All public spaces, galleries, and the central atrium are free to enter

annualGVA.source: Estimated from published tariffs (£22/day cars, £48/day coaches) at assumed occupancy
